title: Week 6: For Loops and the range() Function
text:
By the end of this lesson, you should be able to:

Explain what a for loop does in plain language.
Identify the loop variable, iterable, and loop body in Python code.
Use for loops with lists, tuples, strings, dictionaries, and ranges.
Use range() to repeat code a controlled number of times.
Use enumerate() when you need both an index and a value.
Use zip() when you need to loop over related sequences together.
Write simple list comprehensions.
Recognize common loop patterns used in data analysis.
Debug common loop mistakes, especially indentation and off-by-one errors.







NoteHow to use this page



Read the explanation first, predict what the code will do, and then click Run Code. Learning loops is much easier when you actively predict the output before running the code.

title: Week 6: For Loops and the range() Function
text:
By the end of this lesson, you should be able to:

Explain what a for loop does in plain language.
Identify the loop variable, iterable, and loop body in Python code.
Use for loops with lists, tuples, strings, dictionaries, and ranges.
Use range() to repeat code a controlled number of times.
Use enumerate() when you need both an index and a value.
Use zip() when you need to loop over related sequences together.
Write simple list comprehensions.
Recognize common loop patterns used in data analysis.
Debug common loop mistakes, especially indentation and off-by-one errors.







NoteHow to use this page



Read the explanation first, predict what the code will do, and then click Run Code. Learning loops is much easier when you actively predict the output before running the code.

title: Week 6: For Loops and the range() Function
text:
Why Loops Matter
Programming is often about doing the same kind of operation many times. In data work, you might need to:

Clean every row in a dataset.
Check every file in a folder.
Count how many observations meet a condition.
Convert a list of raw values into a new list of cleaned values.
Search through text for words, names, locations, or patterns.
Build a summary from many individual records.

A loop lets you express the idea: for each thing in this collection, do something.
Without loops, you would have to repeat yourself manually. That is slow, error-prone, and impossible once your data gets large.

title: Week 6: For Loops and the range() Function
text:
What Is a for Loop?
A for loop repeats a block of code once for each item in a collection.
The general structure is:
for item in collection:
    # do something with item
There are three important parts:



Part
Meaning




item
A temporary variable name for the current value


collection
The thing being looped over


indented block
The code that runs once per item









TipPlain-language translation



Read this code:
for fruit in fruits:
    print(fruit)
as:

For each fruit in the collection called fruits, print that fruit.

title: Week 6: For Loops and the range() Function
section: First Example: Looping Over a List
text:
First Example: Looping Over a List
A list stores multiple values in order. A for loop can visit each value one at a time.
Before running the code, predict the output.

fruits = ["apple", "banana", "cherry"]

for fruit in fruits:
    print(fruit)


Run Code



The loop variable fruit takes on one value at a time:

First iteration: fruit = "apple"
Second iteration: fruit = "banana"
Third iteration: fruit = "cherry"

After the third item, the list is exhausted, so the loop stops.

title: Week 6: For Loops and the range() Function
Week 6: For Loops and the range() Function
section: The Loop Body Must Be Indented
text:
The Loop Body Must Be Indented
The indented lines belong to the loop. The unindented lines run after the loop is finished.

fruits = ["apple", "banana", "cherry"]

for fruit in fruits:
    print("Inside the loop:", fruit)

print("The loop is finished.")


Run Code



Notice that the final print() statement only runs once because it is not indented under the loop.

title: Week 6: For Loops and the range() Function
text:
Common Mistake: Incorrect Indentation
Python uses indentation to understand structure. This is different from many other programming languages.
This code is wrong because the loop body is not indented:
for fruit in fruits:
print(fruit)
Python expects an indented block after the colon.






WarningDebugging clue



If you see an error like IndentationError, check whether the lines after your for statement are indented consistently.

title: Week 6: For Loops and the range() Function
text:
Iterating Over Tuples
A tuple is similar to a list, but it cannot be changed after it is created.
You can loop over a tuple the same way you loop over a list.

numbers = (1, 2, 3)

for num in numbers:
    print(num)


Run Code



For basic looping, lists and tuples behave similarly. The major difference is that lists are mutable, while tuples are immutable.

title: Week 6: For Loops and the range() Function
text:
Iterating Over Strings
A string is also iterable. When you loop over a string, Python gives you one character at a time.

for letter in "GEOINT":
    print(letter)


Run Code



This is useful when you need to inspect text character by character.

title: Week 6: For Loops and the range() Function
section: Example: Counting Letters in a Word
text:
Example: Counting Letters in a Word
This example counts how many times the letter a appears in a word.

word = "analysis"
count = 0

for letter in word:
    if letter == "a":
        count += 1

print(count)


Run Code



The variable count starts at 0. Every time the loop finds an a, it adds 1.

title: Week 6: For Loops and the range() Function
text:
Iterating Over Dictionaries
A dictionary stores key-value pairs.
For example:
student_scores = {
    "Alice": 90,
    "Bob": 85,
    "Charlie": 92
}
In this dictionary:

The keys are student names.
The values are scores.

Use .items() when you want both the key and the value.

student_scores = {
    "Alice": 90,
    "Bob": 85,
    "Charlie": 92
}

for name, score in student_scores.items():
    print(f"{name}: {score}")


Run Code

title: Week 6: For Loops and the range() Function
Week 6: For Loops and the range() Function
text:
The range() Function
The range() function creates a sequence of numbers.
It is commonly used when you want a loop to run a specific number of times.
Common patterns:
range(m)          # 0 to m - 1
range(n, m)       # n to m - 1
range(n, m, step) # n to m - 1 by step






ImportantKey idea



range() stops before the ending number.
So range(5) gives:
0, 1, 2, 3, 4
It does not include 5.

title: Week 6: For Loops and the range() Function
text:
range(m): Start at 0
When range() receives one argument, it starts at 0 and stops before that number.

for i in range(5):
    print(i)


Run Code



This loop runs five times, but the values are 0, 1, 2, 3, 4.

title: Week 6: For Loops and the range() Function
section: range(n, m): Choose a Start and Stop
text:
range(n, m): Choose a Start and Stop
When range() receives two arguments, the first is the starting value and the second is the stopping value.

for i in range(1, 6):
    print(i)


Run Code



This produces 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 because the loop stops before 6.

title: Week 6: For Loops and the range() Function
section: range(n, m, step): Choose the Step Size
text:
range(n, m, step): Choose the Step Size
The third argument controls how much the value changes each time.

for i in range(0, 10, 2):
    print(i)


Run Code



This loop counts by 2.

title: Week 6: For Loops and the range() Function
text:
Counting Backward with range()
The step can also be negative.

for i in range(5, 0, -1):
    print(i)

print("Blast off!")


Run Code



Because the stop value is not included, range(5, 0, -1) stops before 0.

title: Week 6: For Loops and the range() Function
text:
When Should You Use range()?
Use range() when you need controlled repetition or numeric positions.
Good uses:

Repeat something 10 times.
Count from one number to another.
Access list items by position.
Create simulated data.

Less good use:

Looping over items when you do not need their positions.

Compare these two approaches.

data = ["a", "b", "c"]

print("Using range and indexes:")
for i in range(len(data)):
    print(i, data[i])

print("\nLooping directly over values:")
for value in data:
    print(value)


Run Code

title: Week 6: For Loops and the range() Function
section: enumerate(): A Better Way to Track Indexes
text:
enumerate(): A Better Way to Track Indexes
Sometimes you need both:

The position of the item.
The item itself.

For that, use enumerate().

data = ["a", "b", "c"]

for index, value in enumerate(data):
    print(index, value)


Run Code



enumerate() is usually cleaner than this:
for i in range(len(data)):
    print(i, data[i])

title: Week 6: For Loops and the range() Function
text:
Starting enumerate() at 1
By default, enumerate() starts counting at 0. You can change that with start=1.

tasks = ["load data", "clean data", "analyze data", "write report"]

for number, task in enumerate(tasks, start=1):
    print(f"Step {number}: {task}")


Run Code



This is useful when creating numbered instructions or reports for humans.

title: Week 6: For Loops and the range() Function
text:
Nested For Loops
A nested loop is a loop inside another loop.

for i in range(3):
    for j in range(2):
        print(f"i={i}, j={j}")


Run Code



The outer loop controls i. The inner loop controls j.
For every single value of i, the inner loop runs completely.

title: Week 6: For Loops and the range() Function
text:
Understanding Nested Loop Output
Think of nested loops as a table.
If the outer loop has 3 values and the inner loop has 2 values, then the total number of combinations is:
3 * 2 = 6

outer_values = ["A", "B", "C"]
inner_values = [1, 2]

for outer in outer_values:
    for inner in inner_values:
        print(outer, inner)


Run Code

title: Week 6: For Loops and the range() Function
text:
What Is an f-string?
An f-string lets you insert variables or expressions directly into a string.

name = "Alice"
print(f"Hello {name}")


Run Code



Anything inside {} is evaluated by Python.

score = 87
print(f"The student's score is {score}.")
print(f"With 5 extra points, the score is {score + 5}.")


Run Code

title: Week 6: For Loops and the range() Function
text:
Formatting Numbers with f-strings
F-strings are useful for formatting decimal numbers.

accuracy = 0.87342

print(f"Accuracy: {accuracy}")
print(f"Accuracy rounded to two decimals: {accuracy:.2f}")
print(f"Accuracy as a percentage: {accuracy:.1%}")


Run Code



This is especially helpful when reporting results from data analysis.

title: Week 6: For Loops and the range() Function
text:
Common Loop Pattern: Accumulation
Accumulation means combining values across iterations.
A common example is summing numbers.

numbers = [1, 2, 3, 4]

total = 0

for num in numbers:
    total += num

print(total)


Run Code



The line total += num means:
total = total + num

title: Week 6: For Loops and the range() Function
text:
Accumulation Example: Average Score
To compute an average manually, we can sum the values and divide by the number of values.

scores = [88, 92, 79, 94, 85]

total = 0

for score in scores:
    total += score

average = total / len(scores)

print(f"Total: {total}")
print(f"Average: {average:.1f}")


Run Code



Python has built-in tools like sum(), but writing the loop helps you understand what is happening.

title: Week 6: For Loops and the range() Function
text:
Common Loop Pattern: Counter
A counter tracks how many times something happens.

scores = [0.7, 0.2, 0.9, 0.4]

count = 0

for score in scores:
    if score >= 0.6:
        count += 1

print(count)


Run Code



This pattern is common when counting records that meet a condition.

title: Week 6: For Loops and the range() Function
text:
Counter Example: Count High Scores

scores = [88, 92, 79, 94, 85, 73, 91]

high_score_count = 0

for score in scores:
    if score >= 90:
        high_score_count += 1

print(f"Number of high scores: {high_score_count}")


Run Code



Try changing the threshold from 90 to 85 and run it again.

title: Week 6: For Loops and the range() Function
section: Common Loop Pattern: Building a List
text:
Common Loop Pattern: Building a List
Sometimes we want to create a new list from an existing list.

numbers = [1, -2, 3, -4, 5]

positives = []

for num in numbers:
    if num > 0:
        positives.append(num)

print(positives)


Run Code



This pattern is useful for filtering data.

title: Week 6: For Loops and the range() Function
text:
Building a Transformed List
We can also build a list by transforming each value.

temperatures_f = [32, 50, 68, 86]

temperatures_c = []

for temp_f in temperatures_f:
    temp_c = (temp_f - 32) * 5 / 9
    temperatures_c.append(temp_c)

print(temperatures_c)


Run Code



The new list contains Celsius values created from Fahrenheit values.

title: Week 6: For Loops and the range() Function
text:
List Comprehensions
A list comprehension is a compact way to create a new list.
Traditional loop:

squared = []

for num in [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]:
    squared.append(num ** 2)

print(squared)


Run Code

title: Week 6: For Loops and the range() Function
text:
List Comprehension Version
The same logic can be written in one line.

squared = [num ** 2 for num in [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]]

print(squared)


Run Code



General structure:
[expression for item in iterable]
With a filter:
[expression for item in iterable if condition]

title: Week 6: For Loops and the range() Function
text:
Filtering with List Comprehensions
This list comprehension keeps only even numbers.

even_numbers = [num for num in range(10) if num % 2 == 0]

print(even_numbers)


Run Code



The condition num % 2 == 0 checks whether the number is evenly divisible by 2.

title: Week 6: For Loops and the range() Function
text:
Conditional Expressions in List Comprehensions
You can also use an if/else expression to choose between two output values.

labels = ["even" if num % 2 == 0 else "odd" for num in range(6)]

print(labels)


Run Code



This does not filter the list. It labels every item.






WarningImportant distinction



These are different patterns:
[num for num in numbers if num > 0]
filters the list.
["positive" if num > 0 else "not positive" for num in numbers]
labels every item.

title: Week 6: For Loops and the range() Function
section: Looping Over Multiple Sequences with zip()
text:
Looping Over Multiple Sequences with zip()
Use zip() when two or more lists are connected by position.

names = ["Alice", "Bob", "Charlie"]
scores = [90, 85, 92]

for name, score in zip(names, scores):
    print(name, score)


Run Code



This is cleaner and safer than manually indexing both lists.

title: Week 6: For Loops and the range() Function
text:
zip() Example: Build Sentences

countries = ["Japan", "France", "Brazil"]
capitals = ["Tokyo", "Paris", "Brasilia"]

for country, capital in zip(countries, capitals):
    print(f"The capital of {country} is {capital}.")


Run Code

title: Week 6: For Loops and the range() Function
text:
Looping Over Nested Data Structures
Many real-world datasets contain nested structures. A common example is a list of dictionaries.

students = [
    {"name": "Alice", "score": 90},
    {"name": "Bob", "score": 85},
    {"name": "Charlie", "score": 92}
]

for student in students:
    print(student["name"], student["score"])


Run Code



This structure is common when working with:

JSON data
API responses
Web data
Intelligence reports
Survey responses

title: Week 6: For Loops and the range() Function
Week 6: For Loops and the range() Function
text:
Controlling Loop Execution with break
break exits the loop immediately.

for num in range(10):
    if num == 5:
        break
    print(num)


Run Code



Once num == 5, the loop stops entirely.

title: Week 6: For Loops and the range() Function
text:
break Example: Search Until Found
This pattern is useful when you are searching for something.

names = ["Alice", "Bob", "Charlie", "Diana"]

target = "Charlie"

for name in names:
    print(f"Checking {name}")
    if name == target:
        print("Found the target!")
        break


Run Code



The loop stops once it finds the target.

title: Week 6: For Loops and the range() Function
text:
Controlling Loop Execution with continue
continue skips the current iteration and moves to the next one.

for num in range(5):
    if num == 2:
        continue
    print(num)


Run Code



When num == 2, that iteration is skipped.

title: Week 6: For Loops and the range() Function
text:
continue Example: Skip Missing Data
In data work, missing values are common. Here we skip None values.

scores = [88, None, 92, 79, None, 85]

valid_scores = []

for score in scores:
    if score is None:
        continue
    valid_scores.append(score)

print(valid_scores)


Run Code

title: Week 6: For Loops and the range() Function
text:
Off-by-One Errors
Off-by-one errors happen when a loop runs one too many or one too few times.
They are especially common with range().

for i in range(5):
    print(i)


Run Code



Remember:

range(5) gives 0, 1, 2, 3, 4.
It does not include 5.
range(1, 6) gives 1, 2, 3, 4, 5.

title: Week 6: For Loops and the range() Function
section: Debugging Strategy: Print the Loop Variable
text:
Debugging Strategy: Print the Loop Variable
When a loop is confusing, print the loop variable at each step.

numbers = [10, 20, 30]

for num in numbers:
    print("Current value of num:", num)


Run Code



This helps you see what Python is doing during each iteration.

title: Week 6: For Loops and the range() Function
text:
Debugging Strategy: Print Intermediate Results
If a calculation is wrong, print the result as it changes.

numbers = [1, 2, 3, 4]

total = 0

for num in numbers:
    total += num
    print(f"After adding {num}, total is {total}")

print("Final total:", total)


Run Code



This is one of the simplest and most useful debugging strategies.

title: Week 6: For Loops and the range() Function
text:
Practice 1: Predict the Output
Before running the code, answer these questions:

How many lines will print?
What is the first line?
What is the last line?


for i in range(3):
    for j in range(4):
        print(i, j)


Run Code

title: Week 6: For Loops and the range() Function
text:
Practice 1 Solution
The loop prints 12 total lines because:
3 * 4 = 12
The first line is:
0 0
The last line is:
2 3
The outer loop goes through 0, 1, 2. The inner loop goes through 0, 1, 2, 3 for each value of the outer loop.

title: Week 6: For Loops and the range() Function
text:
Practice 2: Modify the Code
Try changing the code so that it prints only scores greater than or equal to 80.

scores = [72, 85, 91, 64, 88, 79]

for score in scores:
    print(score)


Run Code



Hint: You need an if statement inside the loop.

title: Week 6: For Loops and the range() Function
Week 6: For Loops and the range() Function
text:
Practice 3: Build a Summary
The following data represents short reports. Count how many reports have the topic "cyber".

reports = [
    {"id": 1, "topic": "cyber"},
    {"id": 2, "topic": "trade"},
    {"id": 3, "topic": "cyber"},
    {"id": 4, "topic": "conflict"},
    {"id": 5, "topic": "cyber"}
]

# Write your solution below



Run Code

title: Week 6: For Loops and the range() Function
text:
Practice 3 Solution

reports = [
    {"id": 1, "topic": "cyber"},
    {"id": 2, "topic": "trade"},
    {"id": 3, "topic": "cyber"},
    {"id": 4, "topic": "conflict"},
    {"id": 5, "topic": "cyber"}
]

cyber_count = 0

for report in reports:
    if report["topic"] == "cyber":
        cyber_count += 1

print(f"Cyber reports: {cyber_count}")


Run Code

title: Week 6: For Loops and the range() Function
text:
Mini-Project: Summarizing Survey Responses
This example shows how loops can summarize simple survey data.
Each response is stored as a dictionary.

responses = [
    {"student": "A", "rating": 5, "completed": True},
    {"student": "B", "rating": 4, "completed": True},
    {"student": "C", "rating": 2, "completed": False},
    {"student": "D", "rating": 5, "completed": True},
    {"student": "E", "rating": 3, "completed": False}
]

total_rating = 0
 completed_count = 0

for response in responses:
    if response["completed"]:
        total_rating += response["rating"]
        completed_count += 1

average_rating = total_rating / completed_count

print(f"Completed responses: {completed_count}")
print(f"Average rating among completed responses: {average_rating:.2f}")


Run Code









WarningThere is a bug in this mini-project



The code above intentionally contains an indentation error before completed_count = 0. Try running it, then fix it by deleting the extra space before completed_count.

title: Week 6: For Loops and the range() Function
text:
Mini-Project Solution

responses = [
    {"student": "A", "rating": 5, "completed": True},
    {"student": "B", "rating": 4, "completed": True},
    {"student": "C", "rating": 2, "completed": False},
    {"student": "D", "rating": 5, "completed": True},
    {"student": "E", "rating": 3, "completed": False}
]

total_rating = 0
completed_count = 0

for response in responses:
    if response["completed"]:
        total_rating += response["rating"]
        completed_count += 1

average_rating = total_rating / completed_count

print(f"Completed responses: {completed_count}")
print(f"Average rating among completed responses: {average_rating:.2f}")


Run Code

title: Week 6: For Loops and the range() Function
text:
Checklist for Writing Loops
When writing a loop, ask yourself:

What collection am I looping over?
What should the loop variable be called?
What should happen during each iteration?
Do I need to keep track of a total, count, or new list?
Do I need an if statement inside the loop?
Should I use enumerate() because I need positions?
Should I use zip() because I need to combine related lists?
Could a list comprehension make the code cleaner?

title: Week 6: For Loops and the range() Function
text:
Common Mistakes and Fixes








Mistake
Symptom
Fix




Forgetting the colon
SyntaxError
Add : after the for statement


Wrong indentation
IndentationError or incorrect output
Indent the loop body consistently


Off-by-one error
Missing or extra values
Remember that range() excludes the stop value


Using indexes unnecessarily
Code is harder to read
Loop directly over items


Modifying a list while looping over it
Unexpected behavior
Build a new list instead


Forgetting to initialize a counter
NameError
Set the counter to 0 before the loop

title: Week 6: For Loops and the range() Function
text:
Key Takeaways

A for loop repeats code once for each item in an iterable.
The loop variable changes value during each iteration.
Indentation determines what belongs inside the loop.
range() is useful for controlled counting.
enumerate() is useful when you need both index and value.
zip() is useful when looping over related sequences.
Loops are often used for accumulation, counting, filtering, and transformation.
List comprehensions provide a compact way to create new lists.
Debug loops by printing loop variables and intermediate results.

title: Week 6: For Loops and the range() Function
text:
Additional Practice Prompts
Try solving these on your own:

Given a list of numbers, print only the negative numbers.
Given a list of names, print only names longer than five characters.
Given a list of scores, count how many are passing.
Given a dictionary of countries and capitals, print one sentence for each pair.
Given two lists, students and scores, use zip() to print each student’s score.
Given a list of words, create a new list containing the length of each word.
Given a list of report dictionaries, count how many reports have priority == "high".
Rewrite a traditional loop as a list comprehension.
